title: Recognizing Actions during Tactile Manipulations through Force Sensing

meta.authors: Guru Subramani, Daniel Rakita, Hongyi Wang, Jordan Black, Michael Zinn, Michael Gleicher
meta.venue: IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS) 2017

blocks.text: This is a generalized method for recognizing different types of tactile force actions from sensor data. This can also be generalized to other types of time series data beyond force sensing. It extracts features from the frequency domain.

This work introduces the first generalized method for robust recognition and temporal localization of tactile force events in human demonstrations using the Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) with the Complex Morlet wavelet.

blocks.caption: System overview: Force signals → CWT feature extraction → Classification → Action recognition

blocks.text: The central breakthrough is representing every sample instance of a force signal with k features that describe the neighborhood around that time point—without any explicit windowing. It provides a representation that machine learning algorithms can use to interpret the underlying force actions.

The CWT with Complex Morlet wavelets effectively summarizes vibration and transient signal phenomena around each time step. Unlike Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT), this approach avoids windowing effects entirely.

items: Each time step gets a k-length feature descriptor from the CWT
Vector-valued signals (force + torque) produce concatenated m × k feature vectors
Every time step can be classified independently
Training requires only N pairs of feature vectors and action labels

blocks.alt: CWT scalogram showing wavelet coefficients over time
blocks.caption: Complex Morlet wavelet scalogram: magnitude of CWT coefficients over time revealing force action signatures

blocks.text: The method was validated using an instrumented set of tongs equipped with multi-axis force sensors, allowing capture of both normal and tangential force components during manipulation tasks.

figures.caption: Instrumented tongs with Optoforce sensors and HTC Vive tracking
Taxonomy of force actions: contact, push, twist, slide, pick, place

items: Sensors: Optoforce multi-axis force/torque sensors (6D → 4D signals)
Tracking: HTC Vive VR controller for 6DOF pose
Data Collection: ROS framework for synchronized recording
Classification: Neural network with 100 hidden neurons (scikit-learn)
Post-processing: Median filter (window=10) + probability thresholding (80%)

blocks.text: The method successfully generalizes across different objects. Training on LEGO block assembly and testing on Nintendo 64 cartridge insertion achieved strong performance, demonstrating the transferability of learned force action representations.

blocks.caption: Cross-object confusion matrix: trained on LEGO, tested on N64 cartridge tasks

blocks.text: The system accurately identifies action sequences in continuous manipulation demonstrations, providing both action type and temporal localization.

blocks.caption: Example demonstration: LEGO block pick-up → fasten → remove → place sequence with predicted vs. ground truth labels

blocks.text: Comprehensive comparison with other wavelets (Derivative of Gaussian, Haar, Real Morlet) confirmed that the Complex Morlet wavelet provides superior performance for force action recognition tasks.

blocks.caption: Performance comparison across different wavelet types validates Complex Morlet as optimal choice

items: Peg-in-hole insertion: Nintendo 64 cartridge, LEGO block assembly
Slip detection: Stylus sliding, controlled slip events
Friction analysis: Dry erase marker cap rotation
Material discrimination: Hard vs. soft object classification
Contact detection: Touch, press, release actions

blocks.text: This work enables learning by demonstration systems to understand not just what forces to apply, but what actions are being performed. This higher-level understanding allows robots to:

items: Make informed decisions when demonstrations are incomplete or corrupted
Leverage past experiences for novel task composition
Recognize and adapt to different interaction contexts
Provide automated analysis and diagnostics for manipulation tasks

blocks.text: Deep learning approaches in imitation learning have just started to use physical signals like touch. Deep learning predominantly focused on vision based learning and mapping to joint trajectories. This approach enables the following extensions:

items: Enables tokenizing tactile actions as input to a transformer-based sequence model
The encoded values generalize well across similar actions so they are great as input to machine learning models
